humanities_role_explanation: Evaluates college-level expertise across arts and humanities disciplines (domain) by leveraging multimodal items like historical cartoons, sheet music, and artworks (content). The benchmark uses these materials to test general-purpose visual-textual reasoning, evaluating them through a standardized multiple-choice framework rather than open-ended hermeneutic analysis.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Combines text and images in a multiple-choice format. It features a novel 'vision-only' setting where textual questions and options are embedded directly into images (as screenshots or photos), requiring models to visually process and holistically integrate both text and figures simultaneously.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Involves factual and stylistic recognition of artworks, identifying architectural systems, music theory analysis from notation, and historical interpretation of political cartoons. These complex visual-textual spatial reasoning and interpretive tasks are ultimately flattened into a 10-option multiple-choice evaluation format.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Evaluates 30 academic subjects spanning Science, Tech, Health, Business, Humanities, and Art & Design.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates state-of-the-art MLLMs including GPT-4o, GPT-4o mini, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Gemini 1.5 Pro, LLaVA-OneVision, and InternVL2.

llm_is_primary_system.note: The primary objective is benchmarking the multimodal understanding and robust reasoning capabilities of MLLMs.

arts_humanities_data.note: Includes subset evaluations in Visual Arts (Art, Art Theory, Design), Music, History, and Literature.

quantitative_metrics.note: Uses classification accuracy for the multiple-choice tasks and Levenshtein distance for OCR similarity.

published_after_march_2023.note: First preprinted on arXiv in September 2024, with updates in May 2025.

english_language.note: The paper and all benchmark datasets are in English.

summary: MMMU-Pro introduces a more robust evaluation framework for the Massive Multi-discipline Multimodal Understanding (MMMU) benchmark to prevent shortcut exploitation by MLLMs. It eliminates text-only solvable questions, scales the candidate options from 4 to 10 choices, and introduces a vision-only setting where questions are embedded directly into images. This design pushes models to truly integrate visual and textual modalities, exposing significant performance gaps in current frontier models.
key_findings: All evaluated MLLMs showed significant performance drops on MMMU-Pro compared to MMMU, with accuracy declines ranging from 16.8% to 26.9%. GPT-4o achieved the highest overall score with 54.0% in the standard 10-option setting and 49.7% in the vision-only setting. Chain of Thought (CoT) prompting showed strong gains in structured reasoning fields but was found to be ineffective or even detrimental in subjective domains like Art and Design.
review_notes: No significant disagreements. Minor differences in the phrasing of analytical tasks and the humanities role explanation were resolved by synthesizing Ciop's critical perspective on the flattening of interpretive tasks into a multiple-choice format with Cip's technical description of multimodal visual reasoning.
paper_name: MMMU-Pro: A More Robust Multi-discipline Multimodal Understanding Benchmark
